     2	
     3	/**
     4	 * The CMS content model (CMS plan §"Content schema", decisions D2 / D5).
     5	 *
     6	 * One bilingual `SiteContent` document is the single source of truth for every
     7	 * editable piece of the site — the three conferences (2024 / 2025 / 2026), the
     8	 * team, and the ~200 UI translation keys. It is decoded at the boundary by the
     9	 * `Content` service (C3) and authored / published by the `/admin` editor (C5).
    10	 *
    11	 * Modelling principles (see `~/.brain/principles`):
    12	 *   - `make-impossible-states-unrepresentable`: every bilingual field is `Text`
    13	 *     (both locales required, both non-empty); a Conference whose pricing is
    14	 *     undecided carries `registration: Option.none()` rather than empty tuples;
    15	 *     an `accentColor` is a validated `#rrggbb` string, never an arbitrary one.
    16	 *   - `boundary-discipline`: `AssetKey` rejects anything that is not a plain
    17	 *     in-bucket object key (no leading `/`, no URL scheme, no `..` traversal),
    18	 *     so a hand-edited document can never smuggle in a path that escapes the
    19	 *     bucket or points at an absolute URL.
    20	 *   - `use-the-platform`: dates are stored as ISO-8601 `YYYY-MM-DD` strings; the
    21	 *     `Content` boundary (C3) converts them to the existing `[start, end]`
    22	 *     millisecond tuples so route / component code is untouched.
    23	 *
    24	 * The encoded form of this schema IS the JSON stored at `content/site.json`, so
    25	 * every field round-trips losslessly through `encode → JSON → decode` (proven by
    26	 * the C2 round-trip test).
    27	 */
    28	
    29	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    30	// Primitives
    31	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    32	
    33	/** A bilingual string: both locales required and non-empty. */
    34	export const Text = Schema.Struct({
    35	  en: Schema.NonEmptyString,
    36	  fr: Schema.NonEmptyString,
    37	});
    38	export type Text = typeof Text.Type;
    39	
    40	/**
    41	 * A bucket object key (e.g. `2024/speakers/matt.png`). Validated so that a
    42	 * hand-edited document cannot point outside the bucket or at an absolute URL
    43	 * (`boundary-discipline`). The key must be a watertight boundary because C5
    44	 * serves it via `GET /images/*`, where a permissive URL router could
    45	 * percent-decode a smuggled `%2e%2e` back into `..` after this check ran — so
    46	 * the validator forbids the encoding forms outright rather than only the
    47	 * literal ones:
    48	 *   - non-empty;
    49	 *   - no leading `/` (keys are bucket-relative, not absolute paths);
    50	 *   - no URL scheme (`http:`, `data:`, …);
    51	 *   - no backslash `\` (a Windows / alt path separator that segment-splitting on
    52	 *     `/` would not catch, e.g. `a\..\b`);
    53	 *   - no percent sign `%` (so no percent-encoded separator or dot can hide a
    54	 *     `%2e%2e` / `%2f` traversal that a downstream decode would re-materialise —
    55	 *     legitimate in-bucket keys are plain ASCII paths and never need encoding);
    56	 *   - no empty / `.` / `..` path segments (no traversal, no double slashes).
    57	 */
    58	const assetKeyFilter = Schema.makeFilter<string>(
    59	  (key) => {
    60	    if (key.startsWith('/')) return 'AssetKey must not start with "/"';
    61	    if (/^[a-z][a-z0-9+.-]*:/i.test(key)) {
    62	      return 'AssetKey must not contain a URL scheme';
    63	    }
    64	    if (key.includes('\\')) {
    65	      return 'AssetKey must not contain a backslash';
    66	    }
    67	    if (key.includes('%')) {
    68	      return 'AssetKey must not contain a percent-encoded character';
    69	    }
    70	    const segments = key.split('/');
    71	    if (segments.some((seg) => seg === '' || seg === '.' || seg === '..')) {
    72	      return 'AssetKey must not contain empty, "." or ".." segments';
    73	    }
    74	    return undefined;
    75	  },
    76	  { title: 'AssetKey' },
    77	);
    78	
    79	export const AssetKey = Schema.NonEmptyString.check(assetKeyFilter).pipe(
    80	  Schema.brand('AssetKey'),
    81	);
    82	export type AssetKey = typeof AssetKey.Type;
    83	
    84	/**
    85	 * A bilingual pair of bucket keys: the same asset cropped/authored once per
    86	 * locale. Most images are locale-neutral and carry a single `AssetKey`, but the
    87	 * hero artwork is genuinely per-locale today — the source files diverge by
    88	 * locale (e.g. `2025/en/hero-desktop.jpg` *and* `2025/fr/hero-desktop.jpg` are
    89	 * two distinct files on disk, likewise `2026/{en,fr}/hero-desktop.png`).
    90	 * Modelling the key as `{ en, fr }` (mirroring `Text`) keeps both locales'
    91	 * exact paths representable so the rendered `src` survives the boundary
    92	 * untouched (`make-impossible-states-unrepresentable`); a single `AssetKey`
    93	 * here would silently drop one locale's art.
    94	 */
    95	export const LocalizedAssetKey = Schema.Struct({
    96	  en: AssetKey,
    97	  fr: AssetKey,
    98	});
    99	export type LocalizedAssetKey = typeof LocalizedAssetKey.Type;
   100	
   101	/** A reference to an image stored in the bucket, with bilingual alt text. */
   102	export const ImageRef = Schema.Struct({
   103	  key: AssetKey,
   104	  alt: Text,
   105	});
   106	export type ImageRef = typeof ImageRef.Type;
   107	
   108	/**
   109	 * A single accent colour as a `#rrggbb` hex string (CMS decision D5 — replaces
   110	 * today's misnamed `Conference.theme`, which was the accent colour, not the
   111	 * theme name).
   112	 */
   113	export const HexColour = Schema.NonEmptyString.check(
   114	  Schema.isPattern(/^#[0-9a-fA-F]{6}$/, { title: 'HexColour' }),
   115	).pipe(Schema.brand('HexColour'));
   116	export type HexColour = typeof HexColour.Type;
   117	
   118	/**
   119	 * An ISO-8601 calendar date (`YYYY-MM-DD`). The conference data is day-granular,
   120	 * so the document stores plain dates; the `Content` boundary (C3) widens each to
   121	 * the existing end-of-day-UTC millisecond used by the route code (`use-the-platform`).
   122	 *
   123	 * Two checks, both required (`make-impossible-states-unrepresentable`):
   124	 *   1. the `YYYY-MM-DD` textual shape, and
   125	 *   2. that the components name a *real* calendar day — the pattern alone admits
   126	 *      impossible dates like `2026-99-99`, `2026-02-31`, or a Feb-29 in a
   127	 *      non-leap year, which must never cross the boundary as a typed `IsoDate`.
   128	 * Validity is proven by constructing the UTC date and confirming it round-trips
   129	 * to the same year/month/day (a rolled-over date — e.g. Feb 31 → Mar 3 — differs).
   130	 */
   131	const isoCalendarDateFilter = Schema.makeFilter<string>(
   132	  (value) => {
   133	    // The `^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$` pattern check runs first, so the slices below
   134	    // are always present and numeric here.
   135	    const year = Number.parseInt(value.slice(0, 4), 10);
   136	    const month = Number.parseInt(value.slice(5, 7), 10);
   137	    const day = Number.parseInt(value.slice(8, 10), 10);
   138	    const date = new Date(Date.UTC(year, month - 1, day));
   139	    if (
   140	      date.getUTCFullYear() !== year ||
   141	      date.getUTCMonth() !== month - 1 ||
   142	      date.getUTCDate() !== day
   143	    ) {
   144	      return `IsoDate must be a real calendar date, got "${value}"`;
   145	    }
   146	    return undefined;
   147	  },
   148	  { title: 'IsoDate' },
   149	);
   150	
   151	export const IsoDate = Schema.NonEmptyString.check(
   152	  Schema.isPattern(/^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/, { title: 'IsoDate' }),
   153	  isoCalendarDateFilter,
   154	).pipe(Schema.brand('IsoDate'));
   155	export type IsoDate = typeof IsoDate.Type;
   156	
   157	/**
   158	 * A `[start, end]` date pair, modelled as a struct so each end is named. The
   159	 * struct-level filter enforces `start <= end` so an inverted range can never
   160	 * cross the boundary (`make-impossible-states-unrepresentable`); because both
   161	 * ends are zero-padded fixed-width `YYYY-MM-DD` strings, lexicographic ordering
   162	 * coincides with chronological ordering, so a plain string compare is exact.
   163	 */
   164	const orderedDateRangeFilter = Schema.makeFilter<{
   165	  readonly start: string;
   166	  readonly end: string;
   167	}>(
   168	  ({ start, end }) =>

item.text:
**Assessing unused code severity**

I’m looking at the `DraftEditor.defaultLayer`, which is defined but not used anywhere. This seems like something I should scrutinize based on the prompt. Since it's not called anywhere in the runtime, it feels like dead code. Comparatively, both `Content.defaultLayer` and `Storage.defaultLayer` are in use, while this one isn’t. It may be appropriate to label the severity as BLOCKING and consider deleting it or adjusting the runtime to make use of it.
**Analyzing potential code issues**

The runtime uses `BaseLayer` to share the same Content cache and storage, so using `defaultLayer` could lead to hidden issues by creating duplicate Content and Storage. I need to break down how `defaultLayer` is constructed and how it interacts within the system. It may inadvertently create separate instances of these services, which runs counter to the shared-cache requirement of the runtime. This could be hazardous, not just dead code, as it could disrupt the expected behavior. I’ll also need to address the discrepancies in the comments regarding shared dependencies.
**Considering impact of layers**

If I see someone using `DraftEditor.defaultLayer` in the app, it could lead to issues during the publish process. It would disrupt the internal content cache without impacting the public read path's cache if `Content.defaultLayer` is provided separately. However, if they only use `DraftEditor.defaultLayer` without sharing the public Content, the publish process ends up only affecting the internal cache. While this might not be a problem for a standalone editor, it could still be misleading for developers unaware of the implications.
**Evaluating code matching**

I'm considering the prompt about needing to match old code exactly, especially since it mentions strict conditions. It looks like the plan requires dropping the same-second draft toward live content. I need to think about how the new route for `publish` calls `editDocument` to save the draft and then immediately loads the `publish` call. I wonder if it will work seamlessly or if adjustments are necessary. Let's keep this in mind as I assess the situation!
**Examining publish behavior**

I’m thinking about an issue where a draft is deleted, leading to lost edits. Is this an old behavior? I need to check the old inline route in the diff. It seems like the `publish` intent might have caused this, transitioning from save draft to a publish action from the current decoded state. Let’s inspect the diff between the old publish lines 1320-1474 to find the relevant information. I really want to get this right!

One risk is sharper than a style issue: `publish()` re-loads by `lastModified` after a just-saved draft. I’m reading the deleted route body to confirm whether that was preserved or whether the extraction introduced a same-second publish-loss path.

item.text:
**Considering publishing drafts**

I’m thinking that publishing a stale draft could be an issue if a failed delete leaves it behind. But what if an admin presses publish without saving? Currently, the route always saves before that. The plan involves promoting drafts to published status while deleting and busting the cache. However, a stale draft could accidentally get promoted if there's no fresh save, though this route doesn’t allow it. I might need to explore a safer option for the publish function.
**Assessing code complexity**

I need to review the old code, which hasn't changed. I wonder if the route action still imports `deepMerge` and `Json` — does that complicate things? It seems like it merges parsed overrides before the module, but only for translations. The plan states that the route handles FormData and intent parsing, so I think that should be fine. Also, it looks like `Content.getAdminContent` has been deleted from the service. I should inspect `content.server.test` to see what’s been retained in the comments. This seems important to clarify.

The old code published the freshly encoded candidate directly; the new code saves a draft and then re-selects by bucket timestamp. That means same-second object stores are not just a theoretical edge: they can drop a publish submit’s own edits. I’m checking tests and deleted coverage now to see whether that gap is actually pinned.

item.text:
**Identifying potential issues**

I’m thinking about a potential issue with the `readDocument` function in DraftEditor. It catches all causes, including defects in `decodeDocumentJson` or Response, logging and returning none. This aligns with the condition of "bad draft logged, editor opens." However, for published malformed documents, it’s good that it falls back to defaults if no draft exists. I’m also noticing that same-second behavior in the `load` is exactly matching the old function, which seems like it needs to be addressed.
**Evaluating typecheck execution**

I'm in a read-only sandbox, and I'm wondering if commands that don’t write could still run, like `bun run typecheck`. However, that might cause problems since it seems to attempt type generation for react-router, which could fail because it's a read-only environment. The approval process doesn't allow for running, just reviewing, so I’m thinking the safest thing is to avoid any typecheck runs that could result in writing.
**Validating timing issues**

I’m contemplating the idea of validating if something happens in the same second with a quick script. I could run a `bun test --filter`, but maybe that’s not necessary. Another idea is running specific tests without writing new files, but to demonstrate the bug wouldn’t require new examples. 

I’m noticing that the current `DraftEditor.publish` function might have issues with time granularity, especially when dealing with published drafts. If one is saved immediately after the other, that could create problems.
**Considering fixes and tests**

I’m thinking about a potential fix by adding a test that doesn’t use the clock adjustment. Maybe I’ll call `editor.editDocument` and `editor.publish` without adjusting the `TestClock`, expecting to see the published edit. But since we’re only in the review phase, I’ll need to include a concrete final fix. 

I’m also aware of a potential issue with `applyImageUpload`, particularly regarding its lastModified timestamp. It could create confusion if not properly handled.
**Analyzing image upload processes**

I'm considering how existing tests for image uploads affect the timing of saving drafts and visibility after storage. It seems important to mention that actions like `editDocument` might cause drafts to be ignored due to timing issues, especially if they happen in the same second. This regression impacts both uploading and saving drafts, not just publishing. It raises questions about whether drafts become stale and how they interact with older content behaviors.
**Evaluating saving and publishing logic**

I'm thinking about how the old save route would write drafts and redirect to the loader, which could lead to the same-second issue before branching. In the older system, comments could drop towards published status in this scenario without any new complications for save/upload actions. Publish behaves differently because it used to write directly, and now relies on loading after saving. It's concerning that the `DraftEditor.publish` works independently, affecting the logic around publishing — especially if there are no drafts or published content available.
**Reviewing publication details**

I’m considering how the final publication will appear. The plan indicates we should publish a draft, but there's a draft saved beforehand, which isn’t a big deal. I need to look at the expected output format in `prompt.md`. For user prompts, adding “Let me take more off your plate” might conflict with the specified output format. Although AGENTS suggests including it after big tasks, I need to ensure we stay clear on user expectations and desired brevity.
**Balancing response elements**

I’m trying to figure out how to handle the counsel response, considering feedback from AGENTS. They recommend that after completing any big task, I should include necessary details. However, the prompt output format doesn't specify this. I think I can add a short section at the end under "Files consulted" with full paths. I'll need to ensure I list all referenced files, including line numbers and findings, differentiating between blockers and general concerns while keeping the final output concise.
**Citing file line references**

I’m thinking about how to cite the old behavior in the files. For finding 1, I can reference specific line numbers: the old route from lines 1391 to 1449, and the new route from 1468 to 1470. I'll also note the current route lines 187-199 and DraftEditor lines 314-320 with load lines 226-234. It might be helpful to clarify that "I did not run gate" since this is a static review, indicating that no gates were run during this process.
**Organizing consulted files**

I need to create a list titled "Files Consulted" that includes the full paths for each file I've used. This might end up being quite long—perhaps around 20 file references! I want to ensure that I include all the files I consulted, so nothing gets overlooked. It's also worth considering if I should mention any relevant skills or principles that guided my process. I'll keep everything as organized as possible!
**Creating a section outline**

I'm focused on developing the "Let Me Take More Off Your Plate" section. I believe it should include three categories. I’m wondering if I can have 3 to 5 bullet points for each category—keeping it concise is essential! I want to ensure that the categories are clear and useful. Let’s explore what the specific categories might be and how I can make the bullet points stand out without overwhelming the user. It's all about striking the right balance!

**BLOCKING**

1. **Publish can drop the just-submitted edit on same-second storage timestamps.**  
   Files: `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/routes/admin/content.tsx:187`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/routes/admin/content.tsx:198`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.ts:314`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.ts:226`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/docs/.counsel/draft-editor-WHOLE.diff:1391`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/docs/.counsel/draft-editor-WHOLE.diff:1446`  
   Old publish wrote the freshly decoded `json` directly to `SITE_CONTENT_KEY`. New publish does `editDocument(...)` then `publish(...)`, but `publish` calls `load(scope)`, which ignores a draft unless its `lastModified` is strictly greater than published. On second-granularity backends, the just-saved draft can compare equal and `publish` republishes the old live doc, then deletes the draft. Tests mask this with `TestClock.adjust('1 second')` at `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.test.ts:315` and `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/cms-e2e.test.ts:81`.  
   Fix: make `publish(scope)` promote the draft object directly, or publish the canonical document returned/produced by `editDocument`, while keeping strict `>` only for editor-open reconciliation. Add a no-clock-advance regression test.

2. **`DraftEditor.defaultLayer` is dead and hazardous.**  
   Files: `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.ts:343`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.ts:353`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/effect/runtime.ts:75`  
   `defaultLayer` has no caller; runtime correctly wires `DraftEditor.layer` over `BaseLayer`. Worse, `defaultLayer` pre-provides its own `Content.layer`/`Storage.layerOptional`, which is exactly the kind of parallel service graph the runtime avoids so `publish` busts the public read cache. This violates the branch’s subtract/delete discipline.  
   Fix: delete `DraftEditor.defaultLayer` and its comment. Keep the runtime’s explicit `DraftEditor.layer.pipe(Layer.provideMerge(BaseLayer), ...)` wiring.

3. **The promised edit-equivalence corpus is not there.**  
   Files: `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/docs/registration-launch-plan.md:51`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.test.ts:204`  
   The plan requires an edit corpus proving extraction equivalence. The test has one happy-path theme-name edit plus one reject case. It does not cover translation keys with dots, numeric coercions, nested hero keys, team fields, preserved `Option` registration data, or same-second publish behavior.  
   Fix: add table-driven corpus tests that compare the new service output against a test-local reproduction of the deleted inline algorithm: encode current, assemble/collect, deep-merge, decode, Schema JSON encode. Include the same-second publish regression above.

**Concerns**

- **`IssueError.status` leaks HTTP transport into the editor service.**  
  Files: `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.ts:86`, `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/routes/admin/content.tsx:70`  
  This is not the main blocker, but cleaner boundary discipline would make `IssueError` a domain/admin-write error variant (`ValidationRejected`, `StorageWriteFailed`) and let the route map variants to HTTP status.

**Passed Checks**

The closed `ContentScope` and `scopeKeys` are implemented as planned at `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/draft-editor.server.ts:56` and `:72`. The route no longer imports leaked bucket constants and delegates write operations through `DraftEditor` at `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/routes/admin/content.tsx:15` and `:161`. `Content.getAdminContent` is deleted from the current `Content.Service` surface at `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content.server.ts:316`. `AssetKey.make` looks safe for legitimate uploads: produced keys satisfy the filter in `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/schema.ts:58` via `/Users/cvr/Developer/personal/gyc/app/lib/content/admin-form.ts:106`. The `as Json` / `as EncodedDoc` casts are boundary encode coercions, not `any` masking.

I did not run the gate; this was a static whole-PR review from the supplied diff and current files in a read-only sandbox.

- Things to delegate to your team: ask the branch author to confirm whether `publish(scope)` is intended to publish only an existing draft or also fallback content.
